rawText:
I am a writer. Writing books is my profession but it's more than that, of course. It is also my great lifelong love and fascination. And I don't expect that that's ever going to change.

But, that said, something kind of peculiar has happened recently in my life and in my career, which has caused me to have to recalibrate my whole relationship with this work. And the peculiar thing is that I recently wrote this book, this memoir called "Eat, Pray, Love" which, decidedly unlike any of my previous books, went out in the world for some reason, and became this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing.

The result of which is that everywhere I go now, people treat me like I'm doomed. Seriously -- doomed, doomed! Like, they come up to me now, all worried, and they say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that? Aren't you afraid you're going to keep writing for your whole life and you're never again going to create a book that anybody in the world cares about at all, ever again?"

So that's reassuring, you know. But it would be worse, except for that I happen to remember that over 20 years ago, when I was a teenager, when I first started telling people that I wanted to be a writer, I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction. And people would say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to have any success? Aren't you afraid the humiliation of rejection will kill you? Aren't you afraid that you're going to work your whole life at this craft and nothing's ever going to come of it and you're going to die on a scrap heap of broken dreams with your mouth filled with bitter ash of failure?"

The answer -- the short answer to all those questions is, "Yes." Yes, I'm afraid of all those things. And I always have been. And I'm afraid of many, many more things besides that people can't even guess at, like seaweed and other things that are scary.

But, when it comes to writing, the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately, and wondering about lately, is why? You know, is it rational? Is it logical that anybody should be expected to be afraid of the work that they feel they were put on this Earth to do. And what is it specifically about creative ventures that seems to make us really nervous about each other's mental health in a way that other careers kind of don't do, you know?

Like my dad, for example, was a chemical engineer and I don't recall once in his 40 years of chemical engineering anybody asking him if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer, you know? "That chemical-engineering block, John, how's it going?" It just didn't come up like that, you know?

But to be fair, chemical engineers as a group haven't really earned a reputation over the centuries for being alcoholic manic-depressives.

We writers, we kind of do have that reputation, and not just writers, but creative people across all genres, it seems, have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable. And all you have to do is look at the very grim death count in the 20th century alone, of really magnificent creative minds who died young and often at their own hands, you know?

And even the ones who didn't literally commit suicide seem to be really undone by their gifts, you know. Norman Mailer, just before he died, last interview, he said, "Every one of my books has killed me a little more." An extraordinary statement to make about your life's work.

sentences.original: I am a writer.
sentences.translation: 저는 작가입니다.

vocabulary.example: She became a famous writer at a young age.

expressions.usage: 자신의 정체성을 선언적으로 밝힐 때. 직업을 넘어 존재의 핵심을 표현.

patterns.pattern: I am a [profession/identity].
patterns.explanation: 자기 정체성을 단순하고 강력하게 선언하는 패턴. 강연의 첫 문장으로 청중의 주의를 끄는 오프닝.
examples: I am a teacher.
I am a scientist.

drillQuestions.question: I _____ a writer.

sentences.notes: 극도로 짧고 단순한 첫 문장. 네 단어로 자신의 정체성을 선언하며 강연을 시작한다. 이 간결함이 오히려 강한 인상을 준다.
pronunciationNotes: writer: /ˈraɪtər/ — "라이터", t가 flap 발음이 되어 "라이러"처럼 들릴 수 있음
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 선언적 오프닝(declarative opening) — 짧고 명확한 정체성 선언으로 강연을 시작

sentences.original: Writing books is my profession but it's more than that, of course.
sentences.translation: 책을 쓰는 것이 제 직업이지만, 물론 그 이상입니다.

vocabulary.example: Medicine is a respected profession.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것이 겉보기보다 더 깊은 의미를 가질 때
당연한 사실을 부연할 때

patterns.pattern: [Gerund phrase] is my [noun], but it's more than that.
patterns.explanation: 직업이나 역할을 소개한 뒤, 그것이 단순한 정의를 넘어선다고 확장하는 패턴.
examples: Teaching is my job, but it's more than that.
Cooking is my hobby, but it's more than that.

drillQuestions.question: _____ books is my profession.
It's _____ than just a profession.

sentences.notes: 동명사(Writing books)가 주어로 쓰이는 구조. "more than that"은 직업 이상의 열정과 헌신을 암시하며, 다음 문장에서 구체화된다.

sentences.original: It is also my great lifelong love and fascination.
sentences.translation: 그것은 또한 제 평생의 위대한 사랑이자 매혹입니다.

grammarElements.text: my great lifelong love and fascination
grammarElements.label: 보어 (명사구 병렬)

vocabulary.example: They became lifelong friends.
She has a fascination with ancient history.

expressions.usage: 오랜 시간 동안 변하지 않는 열정이나 애착을 표현

patterns.pattern: It is also my [adjective] [noun A] and [noun B].
patterns.explanation: 앞선 정의에 감정적 의미를 추가하는 패턴. also로 확장하고 두 명사를 병렬.
examples: It is also my greatest joy and inspiration.
It is also my deepest passion and purpose.

drillQuestions.question: It is also my great _____ love.

sentences.notes: "love and fascination"의 병렬 구조로 글쓰기에 대한 감정의 깊이를 표현. lifelong은 life + long의 합성어로 "평생에 걸친"이라는 뜻.

sentences.original: And I don't expect that that's ever going to change.
sentences.translation: 그리고 그것이 변할 거라고는 생각하지 않습니다.

grammarElements.text: that that's ever going to change
grammarElements.label: that절 (목적절) — 첫 that은 접속사, 두 번째 that은 지시대명사

vocabulary.example: I didn't expect such a warm welcome.

expressions.usage: 미래에 대한 부정적 예상을 부드럽게 표현
변화의 가능성을 완전히 부정할 때

patterns.pattern: I don't expect that [subject] is ever going to [verb].
patterns.explanation: 미래의 변화를 부정하는 패턴. 현재 상태의 영속성을 강조.
examples: I don't expect that my passion is ever going to fade.
I don't expect that this trend is ever going to reverse.

drillQuestions.question: I don't _____ that that's ever going to change.
That's _____ going to change.

sentences.notes: "that that's"에서 첫 번째 that은 접속사(~라는 것을), 두 번째 that은 지시대명사(그것이)이다. 같은 단어가 연속으로 두 번 나오지만 역할이 다르다. 이 구분이 이 문장의 핵심 문법 포인트.
pronunciationNotes: that that's: 자연 발화에서 첫 번째 that은 약하게(/ðət/), 두 번째는 강하게(/ðæts/) 발음

sentences.original: But, that said, something kind of peculiar has happened recently in my life and in my career, which has caused me to have to recalibrate my whole relationship with this work.
sentences.translation: 하지만, 그렇긴 해도, 최근 제 삶과 경력에서 좀 특이한 일이 일어났는데, 이 일 때문에 이 작업과의 관계 전체를 재조정해야 했습니다.

grammarElements.text: recently in my life and in my career
grammarElements.label: 시간/장소 부사구

grammarElements.text: which has caused me to have to recalibrate my whole relationship with this work
grammarElements.label: 계속적 관계대명사절 (which)

vocabulary.example: There was a peculiar smell in the room.
We need to recalibrate our strategy.

expressions.usage: 이전 발언을 인정하면서도 새로운 방향으로 전환할 때
정도를 약화시키는 구어적 표현. 공식적 글에서는 피해야 함

patterns.pattern: That said, something [adjective] has happened, which has caused me to [verb].
patterns.explanation: 양보한 뒤 최근의 변화와 그 결과를 연결하는 복합 패턴. 내러티브 전환에 사용.
examples: That said, something unexpected has happened, which has caused me to rethink everything.
That said, something remarkable has occurred, which has forced me to adapt.

drillQuestions.question: Something kind of _____ has happened recently.
It caused me to have to _____ my relationship with this work.

sentences.notes: "caused me to have to recalibrate"는 사역동사 cause + 목적어 + to부정사 구조 안에 "have to"가 삽입된 복합 구조. recalibrate는 re(다시) + calibrate(교정하다)의 합성어로, 관계를 "재조정"한다는 비유적 의미.

koreanAlignment.en: in my life and in my career
which has caused me to have to recalibrate
my whole relationship with this work

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 전환(transition) — "that said"로 도입부에서 본론으로 자연스럽게 전환

sentences.original: And the peculiar thing is that I recently wrote this book, this memoir called "Eat, Pray, Love" which, decidedly unlike any of my previous books, went out in the world for some reason, and became this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing.
sentences.translation: 그 특이한 점은 최근 제가 이 책, "먹고 기도하고 사랑하라"라는 회고록을 썼는데, 이전 책들과는 확연히 달리 어떤 이유에서인지 세상에 나가서 이 거대하고 초대박이 된 국제적 베스트셀러가 되었다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: that I recently wrote this book
grammarElements.label: that절 (보어)

grammarElements.text: which, decidedly unlike any of my previous books, went out in the world
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (삽입구 포함)

grammarElements.text: and became this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing
grammarElements.label: 등위절

vocabulary.example: She published a memoir about her childhood.
This approach is decidedly different from the last one.
The movie became a mega-sensation overnight.

expressions.usage: 놀라운 사실을 도입할 때
작품이 출판되어 대중에게 알려질 때
원인이 불명확하거나 겸손하게 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: The [adjective] thing is that [subject] [verb] ..., which [result].
patterns.explanation: 핵심 사실을 도입하고 관계대명사절로 그 결과를 설명하는 서사적 패턴.
examples: The amazing thing is that we launched the product, which immediately became a hit.
The surprising thing is that he applied for the job, which led to his whole career change.

drillQuestions.question: The peculiar _____ is that I wrote this book.
It was _____ unlike any of my previous books.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 매우 길고 구어적 요소가 풍부하다. "this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing"에서 this + 형용사 나열 + thing은 구어체에서 흔한 비격식 강조법. 실제 문법적으로는 비표준이지만 TED 강연의 자연스러운 대화 톤을 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: decidedly unlike any of my previous books
went out in the world for some reason
became this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing

sentences.original: The result of which is that everywhere I go now, people treat me like I'm doomed.
sentences.translation: 그 결과 이제 어디를 가든, 사람들은 저를 마치 운명이 다한 것처럼 대합니다.

grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사 which가 앞 문장 전체를 받음)

vocabulary.example: The project seemed doomed from the start.
They treat their employees with respect.

expressions.usage: 앞선 상황의 결과를 형식적으로 도입할 때
과장된 반응을 유머러스하게 묘사할 때

patterns.pattern: The result of which is that [everywhere/whenever] [subject] [verb], people [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 결과를 도입하고 보편적 상황을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: The result of which is that everywhere she goes, people recognize her.
The result of which is that whenever he speaks, people listen.

drillQuestions.question: People treat me _____ I'm doomed.
The result _____ which is that people treat me differently.

sentences.notes: "doomed"는 구어에서 과장된 유머를 만드는 데 사용. Gilbert은 자신의 상황을 희극적으로 묘사하여 청중의 웃음을 유도한다. "everywhere I go"는 복합관계부사로 "어디를 가든"이라는 의미.

koreanAlignment.en: The result of which is that

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기 비하 유머(self-deprecating humor) — 성공 후의 기대를 "doomed"로 과장하여 웃음을 유도

sentences.original: Seriously -- doomed, doomed!
sentences.translation: 정말로요 -- 운명이 다한, 끝장난!

vocabulary.example: Seriously, we need to talk about this.

expressions.usage: 과장이 아님을 강조하거나, 역설적으로 유머를 더할 때

patterns.pattern: Seriously -- [word], [word]!
patterns.explanation: 단어 반복으로 감정적 강조를 만드는 구어 패턴. 유머러스한 과장에 사용.
examples: Seriously -- gone, gone!
Seriously -- finished, finished!

drillQuestions.question: _____ -- doomed, doomed!

sentences.notes: 세 단어만으로 이루어진 극단적으로 짧은 문장. doomed의 반복은 과장된 유머 효과를 만든다. 대시(--)로 끊어진 운율은 코미디 타이밍을 표현.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반복(repetition) + 과장법(hyperbole) — "doomed"를 반복하여 희극적 효과를 극대화

sentences.original: Like, they come up to me now, all worried, and they say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that?"
sentences.translation: 있잖아요, 사람들이 걱정하며 다가와서 이렇게 말해요, "그 책을 넘어서지 못할까 봐 두렵지 않으세요?"

grammarElements.text: Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that?
grammarElements.label: 직접 인용 (부정 의문문)

vocabulary.example: A stranger came up to me on the street.
It's hard to top last year's performance.

expressions.usage: all + 형용사/분사로 상태를 강조하는 구어적 표현

expressions.phrase: Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to
expressions.usage: 부정 의문문으로 상대의 두려움을 가정하며 묻는 표현

patterns.pattern: Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to [verb] that?
patterns.explanation: 부정 의문문으로 두려움을 가정하며 묻는 패턴. 화자가 "당연히 두려울 것"이라는 전제를 깔고 있다.
examples: Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to match that?
Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to repeat that success?

drillQuestions.question: Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to _____ that?
They come _____ to me now, all worried.

sentences.notes: "Like"는 현대 구어에서 담화표지(filler)로 매우 흔하게 사용. "top that"에서 top은 동사로 "능가하다"라는 의미. "all worried"의 all은 "completely"의 의미로 구어적 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: Like, they come up to me now
Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that?
koreanAlignment.ko: "그 책을 넘어서지 못할까 봐 두렵지 않으세요?"

sentences.original: So that's reassuring, you know.
sentences.translation: 정말 안심되죠, 있잖아요.

vocabulary.example: The doctor gave us some reassuring news.

expressions.usage: 아이러니하게(반어적으로) 사용하여 실제로는 반대 감정을 표현
청중과의 유대감을 형성하는 담화표지

patterns.pattern: So that's [adjective], you know.
patterns.explanation: 반어적 논평으로 유머를 만드는 짧은 패턴. 실제 의미는 형용사의 반대.
examples: So that's comforting, you know.
So that's encouraging, you know.

drillQuestions.question: So that's _____, you know.

sentences.notes: 전형적인 반어법(irony). 앞에서 사람들이 "당신은 끝장났다"고 말한다는 이야기 직후에 "안심되죠"라고 하므로, 청중은 이것이 정반대의 의미임을 알고 웃는다. 이 한 문장에 Gilbert의 유머 스타일이 응축되어 있다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반어법(irony/sarcasm) — "안심되는"이라는 말로 정반대의 감정을 표현하여 유머를 만듦

sentences.original: But it would be worse, except for that I happen to remember that over 20 years ago, when I was a teenager, when I first started telling people that I wanted to be a writer, I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction.
sentences.translation: 하지만 더 나빴을 텐데, 다행히 20여 년 전 10대 시절, 처음으로 사람들에게 작가가 되고 싶다고 말했을 때 똑같은 종류의 두려움에 기반한 반응을 받았던 것을 기억하고 있어서요.

grammarElements.text: when I was a teenager, when I first started telling people
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — when x2)

grammarElements.text: that I wanted to be a writer
grammarElements.label: that절 (telling의 내용)

grammarElements.text: I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction
grammarElements.label: 수동태 주절

vocabulary.example: Fear-based decisions often lead to poor outcomes.
I happen to know the answer.

expressions.usage: 상황을 완화시키는 예외를 도입할 때
수동적으로 반응이나 대응을 받을 때. be met with + 명사

patterns.pattern: It would be worse, except for that I happen to [verb] that ...
patterns.explanation: 가정법으로 최악의 상황을 제시한 뒤 예외를 도입하는 패턴.
examples: It would be scarier, except for that I happen to know that this has happened before.
It would be hopeless, except for that I happen to believe that things can change.

drillQuestions.question: I happen _____ remember that experience.
I was met _____ this same sort of fear-based reaction.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 매우 길고 복합적인 구조. that이 3번 등장: (1) except for that (접속사), (2) remember that (목적절), (3) telling people that (목적절). 구어체에서 이렇게 긴 문장을 자연스럽게 구사하는 것이 Gilbert의 화술 특징.

koreanAlignment.en: over 20 years ago, when I was a teenager
when I first started telling people
that I wanted to be a writer
I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction
koreanAlignment.ko: 똑같은 종류의 두려움 기반 반응을 받았습니다

pronunciationNotes: fear-based: 하이픈으로 연결된 복합 형용사, "피어베이스드"로 하나의 단위처럼 발음

sentences.original: And people would say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to have any success? Aren't you afraid the humiliation of rejection will kill you?"
sentences.translation: 그리고 사람들은 이렇게 말하곤 했습니다, "성공하지 못할까 봐 두렵지 않아? 거절의 굴욕이 당신을 죽일까 봐 두렵지 않아?"

grammarElements.text: Aren't you afraid you're never going to have any success?
grammarElements.label: 직접 인용 (부정 의문문 1)

grammarElements.text: Aren't you afraid the humiliation of rejection will kill you?
grammarElements.label: 직접 인용 (부정 의문문 2)

vocabulary.example: He suffered the humiliation of being fired publicly.
The fear of rejection held her back.

expressions.usage: would로 과거의 반복적 행동을 표현. used to와 유사
추상적 감정을 구체적 명사구로 표현

patterns.pattern: Aren't you afraid [subject] will [verb]?
patterns.explanation: 부정 의문문으로 두려움을 가정하여 묻는 패턴. "당연히 두려울 것"이라는 전제가 깔려 있다.
examples: Aren't you afraid the competition will crush you?
Aren't you afraid the critics will destroy your work?

drillQuestions.question: Aren't you afraid the _____ of rejection will kill you?
People _____ say that to me all the time.

sentences.notes: "would say"는 과거의 반복적 행동을 나타내는 would. "Aren't you afraid..."의 반복은 당시 사람들이 했던 말을 생생하게 재현하며, 앞 문단의 현재 반응과 평행을 이룬다.

koreanAlignment.en: you're never going to have any success
the humiliation of rejection will kill you

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 평행 구조(parallelism) — "Aren't you afraid..."의 반복으로 두려움의 양상을 나열

sentences.original: The answer -- the short answer to all those questions is, "Yes."
sentences.translation: 대답은 -- 그 모든 질문에 대한 짧은 대답은 "네"입니다.

grammarElements.text: the short answer to all those questions
grammarElements.label: 주어 (자기 수정 후)

vocabulary.example: The short answer is no.

expressions.usage: 복잡한 질문에 직접적으로 답할 때. 보통 길고 복잡한 진짜 답변이 뒤에 올 수 있음을 암시

patterns.pattern: The answer -- the [adjective] answer to [X] is, "[Y]."
patterns.explanation: 자기 수정(self-correction)으로 답을 정제하는 패턴. 대시로 끊고 더 정확한 표현으로 수정.
examples: The plan -- the real plan for this project is to start over.
The reason -- the main reason for the delay is funding.

drillQuestions.question: The short _____ to all those questions is "Yes."

sentences.notes: 대시(--)를 사용한 자기 수정(self-correction)은 구어체의 자연스러운 특징. "The answer"로 시작한 뒤 "the short answer"로 수정하여 더 정확하게 표현. 이 기법은 연설에서 자연스러움과 솔직함을 전달.

koreanAlignment.en: the short answer to all those questions
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 모든 질문에 대한 짧은 대답은

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기 수정(self-correction) — 대시로 멈추고 더 정확한 표현으로 수정하여 솔직함과 자연스러움 전달

sentences.original: Yes, I'm afraid of all those things. And I always have been.
sentences.translation: 네, 저는 그 모든 것이 두렵습니다. 그리고 늘 그래왔습니다.

vocabulary.example: Many people are afraid of public speaking.

expressions.usage: 현재완료의 축약으로, 뒤에 올 형용사(afraid)가 생략된 형태

patterns.pattern: I'm [adjective] of [X]. And I always have been.
patterns.explanation: 현재 감정을 고백하고, 과거부터 지속되어 왔음을 현재완료로 강조하는 패턴.
examples: I'm passionate about music. And I always have been.
I'm skeptical of easy answers. And I always have been.

drillQuestions.question: I'm afraid _____ all those things.
And I always _____ been.

sentences.notes: "I always have been"에서 afraid가 생략되어 있다. 영어에서는 반복을 피하기 위해 이전 문장의 보어를 생략할 수 있다. 전체 형태는 "I always have been afraid of all those things."

koreanAlignment.en: Yes, I'm afraid of all those things
koreanAlignment.ko: 네, 저는 그 모든 것이 두렵습니다

sentences.original: And I'm afraid of many, many more things besides that people can't even guess at, like seaweed and other things that are scary.
sentences.translation: 그리고 사람들이 짐작조차 못 할 훨씬 더 많은 것들도 두렵습니다, 해초 같은 것들이나 다른 무서운 것들 말이에요.

grammarElements.text: that people can't even guess at
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — things를 수식)

grammarElements.text: like seaweed and other things that are scary
grammarElements.label: 예시구 (like)

vocabulary.example: I can only guess at what happened.
Seaweed is commonly used in Asian cuisine.
There are other problems besides this one.

expressions.usage: 완전히 예측 불가능한 것을 강조할 때
many의 반복으로 양을 과장하여 강조

patterns.pattern: I'm afraid of many, many more things that people can't even [verb].
patterns.explanation: 두려움의 범위를 과장하면서 유머를 만드는 패턴. many 반복 + 예상치 못한 예시로 웃음 유도.
examples: There are many, many more risks that we can't even imagine.
She knows many, many more secrets that people can't even suspect.

drillQuestions.question: People can't even guess _____ what I'm afraid of.
I'm afraid of _____, many more things.

sentences.notes: "seaweed"라는 예상치 못한 예시를 끼워 넣어 웃음을 유발하는 기법. 진지한 두려움의 나열 뒤에 뜬금없이 "해초"를 언급하여 긴장을 풀어준다. 이것이 Gilbert 특유의 유머 스타일.

koreanAlignment.en: I'm afraid of many, many more things
that people can't even guess at
and other things that are scary

pronunciationNotes: besides: /bɪˈsaɪdz/ — "비사이즈", beside(~옆에)와 다르게 s가 붙으면 "~외에"라는 뜻
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 역전된 클라이맥스(bathos) — 심각한 두려움 뒤에 "해초"를 배치하여 의도적으로 긴장을 무너뜨리는 유머

sentences.original: But, when it comes to writing, the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately, and wondering about lately, is why?
sentences.translation: 하지만, 글쓰기에 관해서 말하자면, 최근 계속 생각하고 궁금해 온 것은 왜인가 하는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately, and wondering about lately
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사절로 수식된 긴 주어)

vocabulary.example: I often wonder about the meaning of life.
I sort of understand what you mean.

expressions.usage: 특정 주제로 한정하여 논의를 집중할 때
현재완료 진행형으로 최근의 지속적 사고를 표현

patterns.pattern: When it comes to [topic], the thing that I've been [gerund] is [question word].
patterns.explanation: 주제를 한정한 뒤 핵심 질문을 던지는 패턴. 강연의 논의 방향을 설정.
examples: When it comes to education, the thing I've been wondering about is how.
When it comes to happiness, the question I've been asking is what.

drillQuestions.question: When it _____ to writing, the question is why.
I've been sort of _____ about this lately.

sentences.notes: "when it comes to"는 매우 유용한 관용 표현. "sort of"는 thinking과 wondering 사이에서 자신감을 약화시키는 hedging(완화) 장치로, 구어에서 자연스러움을 더한다. 긴 주어부 끝에 갑자기 나오는 짧은 "why?"가 강렬한 인상.

koreanAlignment.en: the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수사적 질문(rhetorical question) — "왜?"라는 핵심 질문으로 강연의 논의 방향을 설정

sentences.original: Is it rational? Is it logical that anybody should be expected to be afraid of the work that they feel they were put on this Earth to do.
sentences.translation: 그것이 합리적인가요? 자신이 이 지구에 태어난 이유라고 느끼는 일을 두려워하도록 기대받는 것이 논리적인가요?

grammarElements.text: that anybody should be expected to be afraid of the work
grammarElements.label: that절 (진주어)

grammarElements.text: that they feel they were put on this Earth to do
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (work를 수식, 내부에 feel + 목적절)

vocabulary.example: We need to make a rational decision.
That's the only logical conclusion.

expressions.usage: 사회적 기대에 대한 의문을 제기할 때

expressions.phrase: put on this Earth to do
expressions.usage: 천직이나 소명을 가리키는 관용적 표현

patterns.pattern: Is it [adjective] that anybody should be expected to [verb]?
patterns.explanation: 사회적 기대의 합리성에 의문을 제기하는 수사적 질문 패턴.
examples: Is it fair that anybody should be expected to sacrifice their health for work?
Is it reasonable that anybody should be expected to know everything?

drillQuestions.question: Is it _____ that we should be afraid of our calling?
They feel they were _____ on this Earth to do this work.

sentences.notes: 두 개의 의문문 연속 사용("Is it rational? Is it logical?")으로 청중의 사고를 자극. "the work that they feel they were put on this Earth to do"는 관계대명사절 안에 feel + 목적절이 내포된 복합 구조. "put on this Earth to do"는 종교적/철학적 소명의 뉘앙스.

koreanAlignment.en: anybody should be expected to be afraid of
that they feel they were put on this Earth to do
koreanAlignment.ko: 자신이 이 지구에 태어난 이유라고 느끼는

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 연속 수사적 질문(rhetorical question series) — 짧은 질문과 긴 질문을 연달아 배치하여 논증의 힘을 강화

sentences.original: Like my dad, for example, was a chemical engineer and I don't recall once in his 40 years of chemical engineering anybody asking him if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer, you know?
sentences.translation: 예를 들어, 제 아버지는 화학 공학자였는데, 40년 화학 공학 경력 중에 단 한 번도 누가 아버지에게 화학 공학자인 것이 두렵냐고 물은 적이 없거든요, 아시죠?

grammarElements.text: once in his 40 years of chemical engineering
grammarElements.label: 빈도/시간 부사구

grammarElements.text: if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer
grammarElements.label: if절 (간접의문)

vocabulary.example: I can't recall his name.
She works as a chemical engineer at a pharmaceutical company.

expressions.usage: 어떤 일이 전혀 일어나지 않았음을 강조
구체적 사례를 도입할 때

patterns.pattern: I don't recall once in [time period] anybody [gerund].
patterns.explanation: 특정 기간 동안 어떤 일이 단 한 번도 없었음을 강조하는 패턴.
examples: I don't recall once in 10 years anybody questioning his dedication.
I don't recall once in my career anybody saying that about accountants.

drillQuestions.question: I don't _____ once anybody asking him that.
He was afraid _____ be a chemical engineer.

sentences.notes: "chemical engineer"가 문장에서 3번 반복되는데, 이 의도적 반복이 유머를 만든다. "recall + 목적어(anybody) + 동명사(asking)"는 지각동사 구문. "once"가 "한 번도"라는 부정의 빈도 부사로 사용.

koreanAlignment.en: in his 40 years of chemical engineering
if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대비를 통한 유머(humor through contrast) — 작가의 두려움과 화학 공학자의 평온함을 대비하여 부조리를 강조

sentences.original: But to be fair, chemical engineers as a group haven't really earned a reputation over the centuries for being alcoholic manic-depressives.
sentences.translation: 하지만 공정하게 말하면, 화학 공학자들이라는 집단은 수세기에 걸쳐 알코올 중독 조울증 환자라는 평판을 얻은 적이 없긴 하죠.

vocabulary.example: The company has a good reputation.
Many great artists were believed to be manic-depressives.
He struggled with alcoholic tendencies.

expressions.usage: 상대편의 입장을 인정하면서 양보할 때
오랜 기간에 걸쳐 형성된 이미지를 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: To be fair, [group] haven't really earned a reputation for [gerund].
patterns.explanation: 양보하면서 특정 집단의 특징(또는 특징 부재)을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: To be fair, accountants haven't really earned a reputation for being wild risk-takers.
To be fair, librarians haven't really earned a reputation for being rebellious.

drillQuestions.question: They haven't earned a reputation _____ being manic-depressives.
To be _____, that is a valid point.

sentences.notes: "to be fair"로 양보한 뒤 화학 공학자와 작가의 차이를 인정하는 것이 이 유머의 핵심. "alcoholic manic-depressives"라는 강한 표현을 "to be fair"라는 차분한 도입어와 대비시켜 웃음을 만든다. manic-depressive는 현재 bipolar disorder(양극성 장애)로 불림.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 절제된 표현(litotes/understatement) — "to be fair"로 시작하여 극적 대비를 의도적으로 절제하는 유머 기법

sentences.original: We writers, we kind of do have that reputation, and not just writers, but creative people across all genres, it seems, have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable.
sentences.translation: 우리 작가들은, 그런 평판이 있긴 합니다. 작가들만이 아니라 모든 장르의 창작자들이 엄청나게 정신적으로 불안정하다는 평판을 가지고 있는 것 같습니다.

grammarElements.text: but creative people across all genres
grammarElements.label: 주어 (확장된)

grammarElements.text: have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable
grammarElements.label: 주절 (확장)

vocabulary.example: What genre of music do you like?
The project was enormously successful.
The political situation remains unstable.

expressions.usage: "kind of"로 약화시키면서 "do"로 다시 강조하는 이중 조절
범위를 확장할 때
확정적 진술을 피하고 조심스럽게 말할 때

patterns.pattern: We [group], we [hedging] do have that reputation, and not just [A], but [B] across all [categories].
patterns.explanation: 특정 집단의 평판을 인정하면서 범위를 확장하는 패턴.
examples: We millennials, we kind of do have that reputation, and not just millennials, but young people across all generations.

drillQuestions.question: Creative people across all _____ have this reputation.
They have a reputation for being _____ mentally unstable.

sentences.notes: "kind of do have"에서 "kind of"는 약화, "do"는 강조. 이 두 가지가 충돌하면서 "인정하고 싶지 않지만 사실이긴 하다"는 미묘한 뉘앙스를 만든다. "We writers"에서 we와 writers의 동격은 집단 정체성을 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: we kind of do have that reputation
but creative people across all genres
have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable
koreanAlignment.ko: 엄청나게 정신적으로 불안정하다는 평판을 가지고 있습니다

sentences.original: And even the ones who didn't literally commit suicide seem to be really undone by their gifts, you know.
sentences.translation: 그리고 글자 그대로 자살하지 않은 사람들조차도 자신의 재능에 의해 정말로 무너진 것 같습니다, 있잖아요.

vocabulary.example: Many people who suffer from severe depression may think about committing suicide.
He was completely undone by the loss.
She has a gift for music.

expressions.usage: 아이러니하게도 장점이 파멸의 원인이 될 때
even으로 예상 밖의 범위를 강조

patterns.pattern: Even the ones who didn't [verb] seem to be [adjective] by [cause].
patterns.explanation: 예외라고 생각되는 사람들조차도 같은 영향을 받았음을 보여주는 패턴.
examples: Even the ones who didn't fail seem to be scarred by the experience.
Even the ones who didn't leave seem to be affected by the changes.

drillQuestions.question: They seem to be really _____ by their gifts.
Even the ones who didn't literally _____ suicide.

sentences.notes: "undone by their gifts"는 이 구절의 핵심 역설(paradox). "gifts"(재능/선물)라는 긍정적 단어가 파멸의 원인이 되는 아이러니를 담고 있다. undone은 undo의 과거분사로, "되돌려진, 해체된, 무너진"이라는 의미.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 역설(paradox) — "gifts"(재능)가 파멸의 원인이 되는 아이러니를 통해 창작자의 비극을 부각

sentences.original: Norman Mailer, just before he died, last interview, he said, "Every one of my books has killed me a little more."
sentences.translation: 노먼 메일러는 죽기 직전 마지막 인터뷰에서 이렇게 말했습니다, "내 책 하나하나가 나를 조금씩 더 죽였다."

grammarElements.text: Every one of my books has killed me a little more
grammarElements.label: 직접 인용 (현재완료)

vocabulary.example: Every one of these students passed the exam.

expressions.usage: 시간적으로 아슬아슬한 순간을 강조

expressions.phrase: has killed me a little more
expressions.usage: 비유적 표현으로 점진적 소진을 묘사

patterns.pattern: [Person], just before [event], [context], said, "[quote]."
patterns.explanation: 인물의 마지막 말이나 중요한 발언을 인용하는 서사적 패턴.
examples: Steve Jobs, just before he resigned, in his last speech, said, "Stay hungry, stay foolish."

drillQuestions.question: Every one of my books _____ killed me a little more.
Just _____ he died, he gave his last interview.

sentences.notes: 이 문장의 구조는 의도적으로 파편적이다. "Norman Mailer, just before he died, last interview" — 완전한 문장이 아닌 명사구의 나열은 구어체에서 서스펜스를 만드는 기법. 메일러의 인용문은 현재완료(has killed)로 누적적 효과를 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: Every one of my books has killed me a little more
koreanAlignment.ko: "내 책 하나하나가 나를 조금씩 더 죽였다"

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 권위자 인용(appeal to authority) — 저명한 작가의 마지막 인터뷰를 인용하여 논점을 뒷받침

sentences.original: An extraordinary statement to make about your life's work.
sentences.translation: 자신의 필생의 작업에 대해 하기에는 놀라운 발언입니다.

vocabulary.example: She showed extraordinary courage.
This museum is his life's work.

expressions.usage: 일생을 바쳐 이룬 업적을 가리킬 때
to부정사의 형용사적 용법으로 발언의 성격을 평가

patterns.pattern: An [adjective] statement to make about [something].
patterns.explanation: 불완전 문장(fragment)으로 감탄이나 논평을 표현하는 패턴. 동사가 없어도 의미가 전달되는 구어적 표현.
examples: An incredible thing to say about your own country.
A bold claim to make in front of this audience.

drillQuestions.question: An extraordinary statement to _____ about your life's work.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 문법적으로 완전한 문장이 아닌 명사구(sentence fragment)이다. 동사가 없다. 이런 불완전 문장은 구어에서 감탄이나 논평을 표현할 때 자주 사용되며, 오히려 강한 인상을 준다.
pronunciationNotes: extraordinary: /ɪkˈstrɔːrdɪneri/ — "익스트로디너리", 5음절, 강세는 두 번째 음절. extra-ordinary가 아닌 하나의 단어로 발음
